title: Business Administration Introduction
description: Identify business administrator responsibilities and configuration drivers. Understand policies, processes, participants, and the Windchill system architecture.

topics.title: Business Administrator Responsibilities

content.text: The Windchill business administrator manages the Windchill environment and applies business rules that govern how product data is created, managed, and secured. The BA role may not correspond directly to an organization's job title - responsibilities are often shared across a team depending on the organization's size and structure.
Six Responsibility Areas

rows: Product Data Configuration
Manage object types, type-specific attributes, numbering and versioning schemes, and rules governing object creation
Product Data Template Definition
Create and manage object templates and context templates that standardize how new objects and contexts are initialized
Access Control and Monitoring
Create and manage access control policies, notification rules, and processes including workflows, life cycles, and CAD viewable publishing
System Behavior
Create profiles for UI visibility (actions, tabs, attributes), configure public info page tabs, saved searches, system preferences, and Visualization services
User Management and Assistance
Manage users, groups, roles, and shared teams; undo checkouts; provide user assistance and troubleshooting
License Management and Reporting
Assign licenses, monitor license usage reporting, run security auditing reports, and define and manage reports

content.text: The business administrator role is not a single job title - it is a set of responsibilities. In smaller organizations one person may handle all six areas, while in larger enterprises these responsibilities are distributed across a team of specialists.
Responsibilities in Practice

Each responsibility area maps to specific Windchill administration tools. Product data configuration uses the Type and Attribute Management utility, access control uses the Policy Administration tool, and user management operates through Participant Administration. Understanding these mappings helps you navigate the Windchill administration interface efficiently.

content.prompt: Match each business administrator responsibility area to its key activities:

pairs.right: Object types, attributes, numbering and versioning schemes
Policies, notification rules, workflows, life cycles
Users, groups, roles, shared teams, undo checkouts
License assignment, usage reporting, security audits

keyTakeaways: The business administrator manages the Windchill environment and enforces business rules across six responsibility areas
The BA role may be fulfilled by one person or shared across a team depending on organization size
The six areas cover product data configuration, templates, access control, system behavior, user management, and license management

topics.title: Business Configuration Drivers

content.text: Windchill configuration is driven by a thorough analysis of the product development process. Before configuring anything in the system, you need a complete understanding of your company's product development stages, the data involved at each stage, and how that data matures over time.
Four Configuration Driver Areas

cards.back: Determine what types of product data your organization creates and manages - parts, documents, CAD models, specifications, and other artifacts that flow through the development process.
Map out the stages your products pass through from concept to release - such as concept, design, prototype, validation, and production. Each stage has distinct activities and approval gates.

cards.front: 3. Map Product Data in Development Stages
cards.back: Determine which product data types are created, modified, or consumed at each development stage. This mapping reveals the relationships between data and process.

cards.front: 4. Identify Life Cycle and Maturity Process
cards.back: Define how each type of product data matures through its life cycle - what states it passes through, what approvals are required, and when it transitions from one state to the next.

content.text: Start your configuration analysis by interviewing stakeholders across engineering, manufacturing, and quality. Each group interacts with product data differently and has unique requirements for how it should be managed in Windchill.

The output of this analysis directly feeds into the Windchill configuration - it determines the policies, processes, and participant structures you will set up. Skipping this analysis leads to a system that does not match how the business actually works, resulting in workarounds and user frustration.

content.prompt: Arrange the four configuration driver areas in the correct analysis sequence:
items: Map your product data in the product development stages
Identify the life cycle and maturity process for each product data

keyTakeaways: Windchill configuration must be driven by analysis of the actual product development process
Four driver areas guide the analysis: product data, development stages, data-stage mapping, and life cycle maturity
The output of this analysis feeds directly into setting up policies, processes, and participants in Windchill

topics.title: Policies, Processes, and Participants

content.text: The business analysis from the configuration drivers leads to a formal specification that answers three fundamental questions: Which actor can run a specific action? On which object? When is the action allowed? These questions are answered through Windchill's policies, processes, and participant structures.
Domain Policies
Domain policies define the rules that govern behavior within a Windchill context. Three key policy types control what happens to objects and who gets notified:

rows: Access Rules
Control which users or groups can perform specific actions (create, read, modify, delete) on specific object types in specific life cycle states
Notification Rules
Automatically notify users or groups when specific events occur, such as an object changing state or being modified
Indexing Rules
Control which objects and attributes are indexed for full-text search, determining what users can find through search

content.text: Life Cycles and Workflows

cards.back: A sequence of states representing the maturity of an object. For example, a document might progress through In Work, Under Review, Released, and Obsolete. Access permissions often change based on the current life cycle state.

Automated business processes that pass tasks and information between participants. Workflows enforce approval sequences, review gates, and notification chains - ensuring the right people take action at the right time.
Users, groups, and organizations that interact with Windchill objects. Participants are the actors in the system who create, review, approve, and consume product data.

Roles identify the function a participant performs (e.g., Reviewer, Approver, Author). Teams map specific participants to roles within a context - for example, John Smith is the Reviewer for a particular product.

content.text: Life cycles and access rules work together - access permissions frequently change based on an object's life cycle state. For example, an In Work document may be editable by the author, but a Released document is read-only for everyone. Failing to coordinate these settings can leave data exposed or locked out at the wrong times.

content.prompt: Match each building block to its role in Windchill administration:

pairs.right: Automated processes passing tasks between participants
Identify the function a participant performs
Control who can perform actions on which objects

keyTakeaways: Business analysis answers three questions: which actor, which action, on which object, and when
Domain policies include access rules, notification rules, and indexing rules
Life cycles define object maturity states; workflows automate business processes between participants
Roles identify task functions while teams map specific people to those roles within a context

topics.title: Windchill System Architecture

content.text: Windchill is a three-tier enterprise application that separates client access, application logic, and data storage into distinct layers. Understanding this architecture helps business administrators know where configuration changes take effect and how different system components interact.
Three-Tier Architecture

rows: Client Tier
Web browser, CAD tools (Creo, SolidWorks), Microsoft 365, Arbortext Editor
Provides the user interface for accessing and interacting with Windchill data and functionality
Application Tier
Java-based Windchill server, web server, content vaults, optional ThingWorx Navigate
Processes business logic, manages sessions, and stores content files in vaults on the file system
Database Tier
Database server (PLM data), administrative directory (user authentication)
Persists all PLM metadata and object relationships; authenticates users against the directory

content.text: Additional Components

cards.back: Generates lightweight viewable representations (e.g., 3D thumbnails, PDF renditions) of CAD files and documents so users can view content without the native authoring application.
Indexes object metadata and content files for full-text search. Allows users to find objects by searching across attributes, names, descriptions, and embedded document text.
An external directory service (such as Microsoft Active Directory) used for single sign-on (SSO) and centralized user authentication across the enterprise.
Connects Windchill to Enterprise Resource Planning systems for manufacturing release. Ensures that released product data flows seamlessly into downstream manufacturing and procurement processes.

content.text: Content vaults store the actual files (CAD models, documents, images) on the file system, while the database stores metadata and relationships. This separation allows Windchill to scale efficiently - metadata queries are fast in the database, while large files are managed on optimized file storage.

content.prompt: Arrange the three Windchill architecture tiers from the user-facing layer to the data storage layer:
items: Application Tier (Windchill server, web server, vaults)

keyTakeaways: Windchill uses a three-tier architecture: client tier, application tier, and database tier
Content vaults store files on the file system while the database stores metadata and relationships
Additional components include the visualization publication worker, indexing server, LDAP directory, and ERP integration

topics.title: Exercise 1 - Create an Organization

content.text: In this exercise, you will create an organization context in Windchill. Organizations are top-level containers that represent companies or divisions. PTC Publications handles technical documentation and training, and you will create an organization context to support its product data management needs.
You need access to the Windchill training server and site administrator credentials to complete this exercise. Sign in as wcadmin with the password wcadmin.

content.title: Create an Organization
content.objective: Create an organization context in Windchill and verify that the organization participant is registered in the system.

steps.action: Launch a web browser and click the Windchill bookmark.
steps.detail: Open the Windchill sign-in page using the bookmark configured for your training environment.
steps.hint: Use the Windchill URL provided by your instructor if no bookmark is available.

steps.detail: The Sign In button opens the authentication dialog where you enter your credentials.

steps.action: Sign in to Windchill as a site administrator: Username wcadmin, Password wcadmin.
steps.detail: The site administrator has full privileges to create and manage organizations, which are top-level contexts in the Windchill hierarchy.
steps.hint: Note: Do not save the password.

steps.action: Expand the Navigator and select the Browse tab.
steps.detail: The Navigator is the left-side panel that provides access to all Windchill contexts. The Browse tab shows context categories like Organizations, Products, and Libraries.
steps.hint: Click the arrow icon or hamburger menu to expand the Navigator if it is collapsed.

steps.detail: The Organizations icon displays the organization contexts available in the system.
View All displays the complete Organizations table listing every organization context currently defined in the system.

steps.action: From the Organizations table, click the Create new organization icon.
steps.detail: The creation icon opens the New Organization dialog where you specify the organization name, description, template, and other settings.
steps.hint: Look for a 'New Organization' or 'Create' icon in the toolbar above the organizations table.

steps.action: In the Organization Name field, type Example PTC Publications.
steps.detail: The organization name must be unique across the system. If you type a name that is not an existing organization participant, an organization participant is automatically created with the same name.

steps.action: In the Description field, type Enterprise publications context.
steps.detail: The description helps other administrators understand the purpose of this organization context.

steps.action: Expand the Template menu and note the available templates.
steps.detail: Context templates can pre-configure an organization with folders, life cycles, workflows, and access policies. Review the available options to understand what templates are provided out of the box.

steps.action: Leave all the other settings with the default values and click OK.
steps.detail: Additional settings include Subscriber, Allow all members to create projects, Restricted directory search, Conferencing ID, and Conferencing URL. For this exercise, the defaults are sufficient.
steps.hint: If a template is pre-selected, you may leave it as the default.

steps.action: In the Organizations table, notice that Example PTC Publications is listed.
steps.detail: The organization should now appear in the table with the name and description you entered, confirming successful creation.
steps.hint: If it does not appear, refresh the page or scroll through the table.

steps.action: From the Navigator, select the Site context.
steps.detail: Navigating to the Site context allows you to access site-level administration utilities, including Participant Administration.

steps.action: Click the Utilities link, then click the Participant Administration link in the Business Administration section.
steps.detail: Participant Administration allows you to search for and manage all participant types in Windchill, including users, groups, and organizations.

steps.action: Click the Add participants to the table icon.
steps.detail: This opens the Find Participant dialog where you can search for different participant types to verify they exist in the system.

steps.action: In the Find Participant dialog, select Organization from the Search for menu and click Search.
steps.detail: Searching for Organization participants returns all organization principals registered in the system. Your newly created organization should appear in the results.
steps.hint: If the search returns many results, use the name filter to narrow results.

steps.action: In the Search Results field, notice that an Example PTC Publications organization is listed.
steps.detail: The organization participant was automatically created when you created the organization context. This participant can be used in access control policies, team assignments, and notification rules.

steps.action: Click Cancel to close the Find Participant dialog.
steps.detail: You do not need to add the organization to any table — this step was to verify the participant exists.
You have successfully created an organization context and verified its participant registration. In later exercises, you will add products and libraries within this organization.

keyTakeaways: Organizations are top-level contexts in Windchill that represent companies or divisions
Creating an organization automatically registers an organization participant in the system
Context templates can pre-configure organizations with default settings, but manual configuration is also possible
